title: SVC Obstruction
oneLiner: Obstruction of SVC flow, usually by tumour (lung CA, lymphoma); causes facial/arm swelling, airway risk, and cerebral oedema.

sections.Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Obstruction: **One-liner:** Obstruction of SVC flow, usually by tumour (lung CA, lymphoma); causes facial/arm swelling, airway risk, and cerebral oedema.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway obstruction** from laryngeal oedema / mass effect – *single finding: stridor, hoarseness, or tracheal deviation.* **Immediate: sit up, call anaesthetics/ENT, prepare for difficult airway.**
- **Cerebral oedema** from impaired venous drainage – *single finding: altered GCS, headache, papilloedema, or seizures.* **Immediate: head-up tilt, dexamethasone, urgent imaging.**
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive dyspnoea (worse lying flat)
  - Facial/periorbital swelling (‘plethora’) and arm swelling
  - Cough, hoarseness, stridor (airway involvement)
  - Chest pain (pleuritic or dull)
  - Headache, visual changes, confusion (cerebral venous congestion)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Distended neck veins (non-pulsatile) and chest wall collateral veins
  - Facial oedema, erythema, conjunctival injection
  - Pemberton’s sign: arm elevation → facial congestion, cyanosis
  - Tachypnoea, stridor, reduced air entry (if pleural effusion or mass)
  - Signs of underlying malignancy: lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly, clubbing
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Malignant SVC obstruction (lung cancer, lymphoma, mediastinal metastases)
- **Less likely:**
  - **Benign SVC obstruction:** central line–related thrombosis, fibrosing mediastinitis (histoplasmosis), retrosternal goitre
  - **Superior mediastinal mass without SVC obstruction:** thymoma, teratoma, aortic aneurysm (pulsatile mass, normal neck veins)
  - **Congestive heart failure:** jugular venous distension but with hepatojugular reflux, leg oedema, bibasal crepitations
  - **Pericardial effusion/tamponade:** pulsus paradoxus, muffled heart sounds, Kussmaul’s sign
- **Splitter:** Pemberton’s sign + absent pulsus paradoxus + clear lung bases favour SVC obstruction over CHF/tamponade.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Sit patient upright. Assess for stridor, tracheal deviation, Pemberton’s sign.
  - Pulse oximetry, ECG (to exclude pericardial effusion – low voltage, electrical alternans)
  - Capillary blood gas if respiratory distress
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, clotting, calcium, LDH
  - Tumour markers: consider β-hCG, AFP (germ cell), LDH (lymphoma)
  - Blood cultures if febrile (line infection)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR (PA + lateral) – widened mediastinum, loss of azygos shadow, pleural effusion
  - **Second-line:** Contrast-enhanced CT chest (with arm injection on the side that is less swollen) – confirms obstruction, identifies cause (thrombus vs mass), grades stenosis
  - **MRI/MRV:** if CT contraindicated; duplex USS of neck veins (not definitive)
- **Special tests:**
  - Venography (rarely needed, only if stenting planned)
  - Biopsy of the underlying mass: CT-guided, EBUS, mediastinoscopy (do only after controlling airway and with senior input; risk of bleeding due to venous pressure)
- **Never-miss test:** **CT chest with contrast** (with coverage of thoracic inlet) – confirms diagnosis and guides stenting/radiotherapy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Sit upright** (head elevated 30–45°) – reduces venous pressure and airway oedema.
  - **Oxygen** to maintain SpO₂ >94%.
  - **Call anaesthetics/ICU** if stridor or GCS <15 – prepare for awake fibreoptic intubation; avoid lying flat.
  - **Consider IV dexamethasone:** 8–16 mg stat (empiric for tumour oedema) – [check local policy] for dose.
  - **Avoid upper limb IV access** (use lower limb/groin for drugs and bloods); no cuts/shaves on swollen arm.
  - **Diuretics:** not routinely helpful; can cause dehydration and worsen thrombosis.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Identify cause:** CT-guided biopsy or EBUS if histology needed (involve respiratory/oncology).
  - **SVC stenting:** definitive first-line for malignant obstruction – rapid symptom relief (<24h). Arrange with interventional radiology.
  - **Radiotherapy / chemotherapy** if tumour histology known (e.g. small cell lung cancer → chemo; lymphoma → chemo; NSCLS → RT).
  - **Anticoagulation:** if confirmed thrombosis (central line–related) – therapeutic LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg OD) or warfarin/DOAC. Do not anticoagulate blind without CT evidence of thrombus.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Definitive treatment of underlying malignancy (oncologist)
  - Stent patency monitoring (recurrence rate ~20%)
  - Line removal if iatrogenic cause
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV normal saline (if required) via lower limb – rate as per clinical need.
  - VTE prophylaxis: anticoagulate if line-related thrombus; otherwise no prophylaxis if no contraindication.
  - Diet/NBM: if stridor or planned procedure → NBM.
  - Observations: NEWS2 hourly initially; escalate if >5 or any increase.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Dexamethasone:** [check local policy] – typical dose 8–16 mg IV stat, then 4–8 mg PO/IV BD; reduce after stenting or RT. No randomised dose evidence.
- **LMWH (enoxaparin):** Treatment dose 1.5 mg/kg SC OD (for line-related thrombus). Deep vein thrombosis protocol.
- **Chemotherapy/radiotherapy:** as per tumour type – discuss with oncology.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Dexamethasone can cause hyperglycaemia, gastric irritation – give PPI cover if history.
  - Anticoagulation in SVC syndrome: high risk of bleeding if biopsying soon – discuss timing with interventionalist.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No change for dexamethasone; LMWH dose reduce if CrCl <30 mL/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving anticoagulation without CT confirmation of thrombus** – may cause haemorrhage into mediastinal mass. Always image first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Airway obstruction** (laryngeal oedema, tracheal compression) – top killer. Call anaesthetics immediately.
  - **Cerebral venous thrombosis / intracranial hypertension** – GCS drop, seizures, stroke-like signs.
  - **Stent migration or occlusion** – recurrent symptoms.
  - **Haemorrhage** from biopsy (high venous pressure).
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent stenosis due to tumour progression (stent restenosis).
  - Post-thrombotic syndrome (if extensive axillary/subclavian DVT).
  - Superior vena cava syndrome recurrence (10–20% after initial treatment).
sections.Prognosis: - Prognosis depends entirely on underlying cause (malignant vs benign). Median survival with malignant SVC obstruction is 6–12 months (worse if lung CA). Stenting provides rapid palliation but does not alter survival. Benign cases (e.g. line-related DVT) have excellent recovery with anticoagulation and line removal.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always sit patient upright** – most effective immediate measure. Never lay flat for examination or imaging.
- **Use lower limb veins** for IV access and blood draws – upper limbs are congested and IV drugs will pool.
- **Organise biopsy only after stenting or airway assessment** – risk of bleeding is real.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never perform blind biopsy** of a superior mediastinal mass – risk of catastrophic bleeding. Always involve interventional radiology/respiratory with CT guidance.
- **Do not give anticoagulation without CT evidence of thrombus** – tumour can cause obstruction, not thrombus. Anticoagulating a tumour mass can cause haemorrhage.
- **Do not delay definitive treatment (stenting/RT) while awaiting histology** – palliation takes priority if symptoms are severe.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening shortness of breath, stridor, facial swelling (especially after treatment), or new headache/visual changes.
- **Follow-up:** Urgent oncology referral – usually seen within 1–2 weeks. Post-stent patients should have outpatient CT at 1–3 months.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/CO or crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained ≥5 despite initial measures
  - Stridor or hoarseness (potential airway loss) – call anaesthetics/ENT
  - GCS drop ≥2 points or new focal neurology (cerebral oedema/thrombosis)
  - SpO₂ <90% despite O₂ (consider CPAP or stenting – involve ICU)
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2, lactate (if >4 mmol/L + signs of shock), respiratory rate >25/min, or hypotension (may indicate tamponade/PE).

rawMarkdown:
## Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Obstruction
**One-liner:** Obstruction of SVC flow, usually by tumour (lung CA, lymphoma); causes facial/arm swelling, airway risk, and cerebral oedema.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway obstruction** from laryngeal oedema / mass effect – *single finding: stridor, hoarseness, or tracheal deviation.* **Immediate: sit up, call anaesthetics/ENT, prepare for difficult airway.**
- **Cerebral oedema** from impaired venous drainage – *single finding: altered GCS, headache, papilloedema, or seizures.* **Immediate: head-up tilt, dexamethasone, urgent imaging.**

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive dyspnoea (worse lying flat)
  - Facial/periorbital swelling (‘plethora’) and arm swelling
  - Cough, hoarseness, stridor (airway involvement)
  - Chest pain (pleuritic or dull)
  - Headache, visual changes, confusion (cerebral venous congestion)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Distended neck veins (non-pulsatile) and chest wall collateral veins
  - Facial oedema, erythema, conjunctival injection
  - Pemberton’s sign: arm elevation → facial congestion, cyanosis
  - Tachypnoea, stridor, reduced air entry (if pleural effusion or mass)
  - Signs of underlying malignancy: lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly, clubbing

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Malignant SVC obstruction (lung cancer, lymphoma, mediastinal metastases)
- **Less likely:**
  - **Benign SVC obstruction:** central line–related thrombosis, fibrosing mediastinitis (histoplasmosis), retrosternal goitre
  - **Superior mediastinal mass without SVC obstruction:** thymoma, teratoma, aortic aneurysm (pulsatile mass, normal neck veins)
  - **Congestive heart failure:** jugular venous distension but with hepatojugular reflux, leg oedema, bibasal crepitations
  - **Pericardial effusion/tamponade:** pulsus paradoxus, muffled heart sounds, Kussmaul’s sign
- **Splitter:** Pemberton’s sign + absent pulsus paradoxus + clear lung bases favour SVC obstruction over CHF/tamponade.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Sit patient upright. Assess for stridor, tracheal deviation, Pemberton’s sign.
  - Pulse oximetry, ECG (to exclude pericardial effusion – low voltage, electrical alternans)
  - Capillary blood gas if respiratory distress
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, clotting, calcium, LDH
  - Tumour markers: consider β-hCG, AFP (germ cell), LDH (lymphoma)
  - Blood cultures if febrile (line infection)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR (PA + lateral) – widened mediastinum, loss of azygos shadow, pleural effusion
  - **Second-line:** Contrast-enhanced CT chest (with arm injection on the side that is less swollen) – confirms obstruction, identifies cause (thrombus vs mass), grades stenosis
  - **MRI/MRV:** if CT contraindicated; duplex USS of neck veins (not definitive)
- **Special tests:**
  - Venography (rarely needed, only if stenting planned)
  - Biopsy of the underlying mass: CT-guided, EBUS, mediastinoscopy (do only after controlling airway and with senior input; risk of bleeding due to venous pressure)
- **Never-miss test:** **CT chest with contrast** (with coverage of thoracic inlet) – confirms diagnosis and guides stenting/radiotherapy.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Sit upright** (head elevated 30–45°) – reduces venous pressure and airway oedema.
  - **Oxygen** to maintain SpO₂ >94%.
  - **Call anaesthetics/ICU** if stridor or GCS <15 – prepare for awake fibreoptic intubation; avoid lying flat.
  - **Consider IV dexamethasone:** 8–16 mg stat (empiric for tumour oedema) – [check local policy] for dose.
  - **Avoid upper limb IV access** (use lower limb/groin for drugs and bloods); no cuts/shaves on swollen arm.
  - **Diuretics:** not routinely helpful; can cause dehydration and worsen thrombosis.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Identify cause:** CT-guided biopsy or EBUS if histology needed (involve respiratory/oncology).
  - **SVC stenting:** definitive first-line for malignant obstruction – rapid symptom relief (<24h). Arrange with interventional radiology.
  - **Radiotherapy / chemotherapy** if tumour histology known (e.g. small cell lung cancer → chemo; lymphoma → chemo; NSCLS → RT).
  - **Anticoagulation:** if confirmed thrombosis (central line–related) – therapeutic LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg OD) or warfarin/DOAC. Do not anticoagulate blind without CT evidence of thrombus.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Definitive treatment of underlying malignancy (oncologist)
  - Stent patency monitoring (recurrence rate ~20%)
  - Line removal if iatrogenic cause
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV normal saline (if required) via lower limb – rate as per clinical need.
  - VTE prophylaxis: anticoagulate if line-related thrombus; otherwise no prophylaxis if no contraindication.
  - Diet/NBM: if stridor or planned procedure → NBM.
  - Observations: NEWS2 hourly initially; escalate if >5 or any increase.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Dexamethasone:** [check local policy] – typical dose 8–16 mg IV stat, then 4–8 mg PO/IV BD; reduce after stenting or RT. No randomised dose evidence.
- **LMWH (enoxaparin):** Treatment dose 1.5 mg/kg SC OD (for line-related thrombus). Deep vein thrombosis protocol.
- **Chemotherapy/radiotherapy:** as per tumour type – discuss with oncology.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Dexamethasone can cause hyperglycaemia, gastric irritation – give PPI cover if history.
  - Anticoagulation in SVC syndrome: high risk of bleeding if biopsying soon – discuss timing with interventionalist.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No change for dexamethasone; LMWH dose reduce if CrCl <30 mL/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving anticoagulation without CT confirmation of thrombus** – may cause haemorrhage into mediastinal mass. Always image first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Airway obstruction** (laryngeal oedema, tracheal compression) – top killer. Call anaesthetics immediately.
  - **Cerebral venous thrombosis / intracranial hypertension** – GCS drop, seizures, stroke-like signs.
  - **Stent migration or occlusion** – recurrent symptoms.
  - **Haemorrhage** from biopsy (high venous pressure).
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent stenosis due to tumour progression (stent restenosis).
  - Post-thrombotic syndrome (if extensive axillary/subclavian DVT).
  - Superior vena cava syndrome recurrence (10–20% after initial treatment).

### Prognosis
- Prognosis depends entirely on underlying cause (malignant vs benign). Median survival with malignant SVC obstruction is 6–12 months (worse if lung CA). Stenting provides rapid palliation but does not alter survival. Benign cases (e.g. line-related DVT) have excellent recovery with anticoagulation and line removal.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always sit patient upright** – most effective immediate measure. Never lay flat for examination or imaging.
- **Use lower limb veins** for IV access and blood draws – upper limbs are congested and IV drugs will pool.
- **Organise biopsy only after stenting or airway assessment** – risk of bleeding is real.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never perform blind biopsy** of a superior mediastinal mass – risk of catastrophic bleeding. Always involve interventional radiology/respiratory with CT guidance.
- **Do not give anticoagulation without CT evidence of thrombus** – tumour can cause obstruction, not thrombus. Anticoagulating a tumour mass can cause haemorrhage.
- **Do not delay definitive treatment (stenting/RT) while awaiting histology** – palliation takes priority if symptoms are severe.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening shortness of breath, stridor, facial swelling (especially after treatment), or new headache/visual changes.
- **Follow-up:** Urgent oncology referral – usually seen within 1–2 weeks. Post-stent patients should have outpatient CT at 1–3 months.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/CO or crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained ≥5 despite initial measures
  - Stridor or hoarseness (potential airway loss) – call anaesthetics/ENT
  - GCS drop ≥2 points or new focal neurology (cerebral oedema/thrombosis)
  - SpO₂ <90% despite O₂ (consider CPAP or stenting – involve ICU)
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2, lactate (if >4 mmol/L + signs of shock), respiratory rate >25/min, or hypotension (may indicate tamponade/PE).
